findings.description:
indent_block uses text.splitlines() then '
'.join(), which drops any trailing newline and normalizes 
/ to 
, so the output is not a faithful re-indentation of the input (e.g. indent_block('a
') returns '  a', losing the final newline).

slugify slices slug[:max_length] with no guard against negative max_length, so a negative value (e.g. slugify('hello-world', -5)) silently slices from the end of the string and yields a wrong, truncated-from-the-tail slug instead of erroring or clamping.

mask_middle multiplies mask_char by the middle length (mask_char * (len(value) - visible*2)); if mask_char is more than one character the output length no longer matches the input length, violating the implicit single-character contract.

mask_middle reveals 2*visible characters once len(value) > visible*2, so a length-9 value with default visible=4 exposes 8 of 9 characters; the 'fully masked when short' threshold leaves near-complete content visible just past the boundary (documented but security-relevant for a masking helper).

slugify's NFKD + ascii('ignore') path drops characters with no ASCII decomposition (e.g. CJK), so fully non-ASCII input collapses to an empty slug with no fallback identifier; behavior is documented ('invalid chars dropped') but worth noting as a usability edge.

No public function validates None/non-str input despite str type hints, so any None argument raises AttributeError/TypeError rather than a clear error; consistent module-wide choice for a 'pure functions' helper, but unguarded at every entry point.
